| Red‑flag symptom | Why it matters |
|------------------|----------------|
| Sudden, worst‑ever headache ("thunderclap") | Can signal bleeding or other acute brain events |
| New neurological changes (weakness, difficulty speaking, vision loss, severe confusion) | May indicate stroke, tumor, or infection |
| Fever, stiff neck, rash | Possible meningitis or other infection |
| Headache that wakes you from sleep or is worse at night | Can suggest increased intracranial pressure |
| Recent head trauma | Risk of bleeding or concussion complications |
| Immunosuppression, cancer, recent sinus surgery | Higher risk of serious complications |
| Progressive worsening over days with no clear trigger | Needs work‑up to rule out secondary causes |

| Feature | Typical concern |

If **any new concerning symptom** (sudden severe pain, neurological changes, fever, stiff neck, vomiting, or rapid worsening) develops, call emergency services or go to the nearest emergency department right away. Otherwise, schedule that prompt appointment and let a clinician evaluate you in person.
